name: Tribal Guardian
pedia: The Tribal Guardian protects your capital against all intruders. The ruler finest men.

name: Palace Guard
pedia: The Palace guard protects your capital against all intruders. The ruler finest men.

name: Kings Guard
pedia: The Kings Guard protects your capital against all intruders. The ruler finest men.

name: Watcher
pedia: Early tribes often relied upon sharp-sighted, wary warriors that watched over the tribe. Despite their strength, they would stay behind with the tribe to guard and protect and to break up any violent conflicts that erupted from within. They didn't answer to anyone in particular except the best interests of the tribe, whatever that would seem to be at the time.

name: Enforcers
pedia: As society complexifies and leadership emerges, those who serve the will of the leaders begin to exert their strength over the people to enforce that will. Usually in primitive societies the will of the leader would serve the best intentions for the people, even if the people didn't always agree.

name: Town Watchmen
pedia: Watchmen were groups of men, usually authorised by a state, government, or society, to deter criminal activity and provide law enforcement. Watchmen have existed in various guises throughout the world and were generally succeeded by the emergence of formally organised policing.

name: Patrols
pedia: In response to the steady organization of criminal enterprises, rigid Patrol units begin to form as a pseudo-military branch of the government. With upgraded weaponry, this new breed of law enforcement is ready to take down all offenders.

name: Guard
pedia: City guard, city watch, town guard or town watch was a law enforcement formation found in some countries and historical periods, usually subordinate to the local municipal government. Historically many cities had their own guard formations, which doubled as police and military force in times of need.

name: Marshal
pedia: The Marshal, city guard, city watch, town guard or town watch was a law enforcement formation found in some countries and historical periods, usually subordinate to the local municipal government. Historically many cities had their own guard formations, which doubled as police and military force in times of need.

name: Exile
pedia: The Lord said, "What have you done? Listen! Your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground. Now you are under a curse and driven from the ground, which opened its mouth to receive your brother's blood from your hand. When you work the ground, it will no longer yield its crops for you. You will be a restless wanderer on the Earth."

name: Bandit Footpad
pedia: As long as there have been footpaths, there have been bandits. Banditry is common in rural, rugged areas of countries with weak militaries, since such nations do not have the means to defend their borders and control the hinterland. Bandits prefer wooded, hilly areas, since such environments provide many hiding places. It should be noted that there are social bandits, different from the run-of-the-mill cut-throats who prey on undefended travellers. Social bandits prey on the wealthy and soldiers of the country they have issue with as a means of protest; such acts are similar in nature to the peasant revolt.

name: Partisan
pedia: A partisan is a member of an irregular military force formed to oppose control of an area by a foreign power or by an army of occupation. The term can apply to the field element of resistance movements that opposed German rule several countries during World War II, or those who after the war fought the Soviet Union's Communist rule in Eastern Europe. (Original mod by GIR and Stolenrays.)

name: Bandit Rider
pedia: As long as there have been footpaths, there have been bandits. Banditry is common in rural, rugged areas of countries with weak militaries, since such nations do not have the means to defend their borders and control the hinterland. Bandits prefer wooded, hilly areas, since such environments provide many hiding places. It should be noted that there are social bandits, different from the run-of-the-mill cut-throats who prey on undefended travellers. Social bandits prey on the wealthy and soldiers of the country they have issue with as a means of protest; such acts are similar in nature to the peasant revolt.

name: Highwayman
pedia: "Highwaymen" is the name given to mounted robbers who preyed on travellers. They were especially known in Great Britain and Ireland, operating from the Elizabethan era to the early 19th century. Highwaymen would work alone, in pairs, or in gangs, and stop horses and coaches for their money and valuables. Some highwaymen later earned reputations as romantic heroes, in the style of Robin Hood. After the development of turnpikes and railways, which made escape for the highwaymen difficult, they disappeared.

name: Ambusher
pedia: The act of lying in wait to attack by surprise. A sudden attack made from a concealed position.

name: Stalkers
pedia: The tactics of ambushing have become a more refined and studied procedure at this stage. The cause for the Stalker's expanse in power and strength is enhanced weaponry and tactics developed by more civilized minds whom have taken time to study and apply more examples of tactics from previous military campaigns and observations of the natural world. It's no longer just enough to jump out of the bushes and attack... now we're setting traps and picking where to stage our attacks with great care. And using bows with poisoned arrows over blowguns does help as well.

name: Assassin
pedia: Although weak at attacking and defending cities, an Assassin's ability to attack the weakest unit in a stack makes it perfect for picking off units.

name: Sharpshooter
pedia: is a person who is skilled in precision shooting, using projectile weapons, such as with a rifle but most commonly with a sniper rifle, to shoot at long range targets. The main difference between a sharpshooter and a normal sniper is that a sharpshooter is usually considered an organic part of a team of soldiers, whereas regular snipers tend to work alone or with other snipers. In the military, marksmen are sometimes attached to an infantry squad where they take accurate long-range shots at valuable targets as needed, thus extending the reach of the squad.

name: Judge
pedia: A judge presides over court proceedings, either alone or as a part of a panel of judges. The powers, functions, method of appointment, discipline, and training of judges vary widely across different jurisdictions. The judge is supposed to conduct the trial impartially and in an open court. The judge hears all the witnesses and any other evidence presented by the parties of the case, assesses the credibility and arguments of the parties, and then issues a ruling on the matter at hand based on his or her interpretation of the law and his or her own personal judgment. In some jurisdictions, the judge's powers may be shared with a jury. In inquisitorial systems of criminal investigation, a judge might also be an examining magistrate.

name: Monk
pedia: Monks are holy men, who work for their god to bring salvation to the people. They do often good things, they care about the poor and weak, but they if they are attacked, they are also strong and able to put up a fight.[PARAGRAPH:2]Concept WarriorsOfGod by The_J, Monk graphics is the Generic Male Missionary by Refar. Made into a module for RoM by Dancing Hoskuld.

name: Treasure
pedia: Treasure (from - thesauros, meaning "treasure store", romanized as thesaurus) is a concentration of riches, often one which is considered lost or forgotten until being rediscovered. Some jurisdictions legally define what constitutes treasure, such as in the British Treasure Act 1996. The phrase "blood and treasure" or "lives and treasure" has been used to refer to the human and monetary costs associated with massive endeavours such as war that expend both.

name: Noble
pedia: Nobility is a state-privileged status which is generally hereditary, but which may also be personal only. Titles of nobility are usually associated with present or former monarchies. The term originally referred to those who were "known" or "notable" and was applied to the highest social class in pre-modern societies. In the feudal system (in Europe and elsewhere), the nobility were generally those who held a fief, often land or office, under vassalage, i.e., in exchange for allegiance and various, mainly military, services to the Monarch and at lower levels to another nobleman. It rapidly came to be seen as a hereditary caste, sometimes associated with a right to bear a hereditary title and, for example in pre-revolutionary France, enjoying fiscal and other privileges. While noble status formerly conferred significant privileges, today, in most Western countries, noble status is a purely honorary dignity. In the United Kingdom, where some peerage titles, until recently, guaranteed a seat in the Upper House of the Parliament, there are still some residual privileges. Nobility is a historical, social and often legal notion, distinct from socio-economic status which is mainly based on income and possessions. Being wealthy or influential does not automatically make one a noble, nor are all nobles wealthy and influential (aristocratic families have lost their fortunes in various ways, and the concept of the 'poor nobleman' is almost as old as nobility itself). Countries without a feudal tradition do not have a nobility as such.

name: Lord
pedia: Lord is a title with various meanings. It can denote a prince or a feudal superior (especially a feudal tenant who holds directly from the king, i.e., a baron). The title today is mostly used in connection with the peerage of the United Kingdom or its predecessor countries, although some users of the title do not themselves hold peerages, and use it 'by courtesy'. The title may also be used in conjunction with others to denote a superior holder of an otherwise generic title, in such combinations as "Lord Mayor" or "Lord Chief Justice". The title is primarily taken by men, while women will usually take the title 'lady'. However, this is not universal, as the Lord of Mann and female Lord Mayors are examples of women who are styled 'lord'.

name: Aristocrat
pedia: Vassalage is a feudal term referring to a person who enters a contract with his or her lord, agreeing to serve and protect the lord in return for monetary rewards or ownership or sovereignty over a parcel of land. The contract is often of both political and religious significance binding both parties by the rules of man and of God.[PARAGRAPH:2]Vassalage is often tribal in nature. One tribe conquers another, and rather than killing the conquered tribe or taking over its direct management, the conquering ruler makes the conquered his vassal: the lower ruler continues to maintain control over his people and territory, while paying tribute to his or her lord in the form of money, goods, soldiers or slaves.

name: Viceroy
pedia: A viceroy is a royal official who runs a country or province in the name of and as representative of the monarch. The term derives from the Latin prefix vice-, meaning "in the place of" and the French word roi, meaning king. His province or larger territory is called a viceroyalty. The relative adjective is viceregal. A vicereine is a woman in a viceregal position, or a viceroy's wife. The etymological allusion to the royal style can create the perception that the office ranks higher than governor-general and lord lieutenant, even in cases when it is a synonym for that administrative rank and not necessarily ranked above "provincial" (lieutenant-) governors. In some cases, the title (and the office, when the title is permanently attached to the job) is reserved for members of the ruling dynasty. It was not uncommon for potential heirs to the throne to obtain such a post (or an equivalent one, without the viceregal style) as a test and learning stage, not unlike the even loftier "associations to the throne", such as the Roman consortium imperii or the Caesars in Emperor Diocletian's original Tetrarchy.

name: Great Hunter
pedia: Hunting is the practice of killing or trapping any animal, or pursuing it with the intent of doing so. Hunting wildlife or feral animals is most commonly done by humans for food, recreation, or trade. In present-day use, lawful hunting is distinguished from poaching, which is the illegal killing, trapping or capture of the hunted species. The species that are hunted are referred to as game and are usually mammals and birds.

name: Shepherd
pedia: The first people to tend to animals and begin learning how their health needs may at times differ from humans were the humble Shepherds. They mastered these skills to the point that when animals were brought to the battlefield it was the shepherds of the community that were called upon to tend to the injured.

name: Cartwright
pedia: Also known as a wainwright. One can imagine that, when the wheel was a new invention, those who could build and repair carts and invent further mechanisms would have been viewed as technological powerhouses, much as we view modern rocket scientists. They would've been expensive to hire, and limited in broad application, but what they did, they did well, and they were necessary for.

name: Veterinarian
pedia: After a time, the distinction of how to care for injured and sick animals and people become more and more apparent and the human knowledge base had expanded to reflect that the care of each had become distinctly differing professions, albeit knowledge obtained in one could be somewhat applicable to the other in many cases.

name: Siege Engineer
pedia: With the advent of more complex machinery, particularly in use as powerful wall-busting weapons of war, the need for intelligent, mathematically educated engineers on the battlefield to assist and repair these new gatecrashers became apparent. For its age, this would've been some of the grandest expressions of human technology and these were the folks who understood all which was not immediately obvious about these powerful forces.

name: Field Veterinarian
pedia: As military forces came to rely more and more upon mounted warfare, not only were there major medical improvements in the field of veterinary medicine, but it became necessary to mount the healers as well so that nation's mounted armies could retain their high mobility without losing access to specialized healing care to support them.

name: Gear Mechanic
pedia: The invention of the gear, along with many other new developments in machinery and parts development pushed the science of engineering to new heights, prompting deeper specialization into the development and repair of many new forms of mechanical wizardry. Those who maintained this knowledge at the cutting edge were often drafted into the service of the state to maintain the most complex equipment.

name: Wise Woman
pedia: As gatherers explored their surroundings and what the results of consuming various native substances they began to learn what would make a person sick, what would provide sustenance, and what would assist in alleviating various symptoms of disease, injury and discomfort. The wise women who had spent their younger years gathering, keeping and sharing verbal records of what was beneficial for various purposes would often be turned to by the tribe as a source of healing and guidance. This role grew in significance as the human mind evolved into more and more advanced forms deepening the capacity for knowledge retention and application.

name: Healer
pedia: The Healer provides his patients with medicinal herbs, which were very effective in healing the allergies, and raising and renewing the body vitality.

name: Medicine Man
pedia: While the term is generally reserved for Native American Shamans and African Witchdoctors, the Medicine Man in C2C represents any and all highly advanced intuitive or spiritual medical practice involving the highest degree of herbal wisdoms and cultural knowledge regarding healing and control of disease, both physical and psychological. The Babylonians had similar 'soothsayers' and seers that would practice medicine as well. Spirituality and medical practice tended to go hand in hand in the earliest forms of developing civilizations.

name: Apothecary
pedia: The Apothecary provides his patients with medicinal herbs, which were very effective in healing the allergies, and raising and renewing the body vitality.

name: Doctor
pedia: At the point that Medical Care begins to become a scrutinized research practice and science, the Doctor was the practicing physician relied upon to deliver assistance where needed. Often Doctors would work house calls and go to the patient if at all possible. Interestingly, often Doctors were also the Barbers of the town.

name: Surgeon
pedia: In medicine, a surgeon is a specialist in surgery. Surgery is a broad category of invasive medical treatment that involves the cutting of a body, whether that of a human or a other animal, for a specific reason such as the removal of diseased tissue or to repair a tear or breakage. Surgeons may be physicians, podiatrists, dentists, or veterinarians. Surgeons usually train for longer than other medical specialists.

name: Immigrant
pedia: City must have a population of at least 10 and will lose 2 population points to train 1 immigrant. The Immigrant will add 1 population point when joined to a city having a population of less than 7. The maintenance cost of 2 gold per turn ends when the immigrant joins a city.

name: Camel Cuirassier
pedia: Even in the age of gunpowder, desert civilizations, especially those of the Sahara, found that the camel's endurance - and its sand-adapted hooves - gave it a slight edge over the horse as a mount for desert campaigns.

name: Camel Cavalry
pedia: Even in the age of the rifle, desert civilizations, especially those of the Sahara, found that the camel's endurance - and its sand-adapted hooves - gave it a slight edge over the horse as a mount for desert campaigns. Of course it has its disadvantages, but if camels are all that is readily available to you, you find them a very competitive substitute.

City must have a population of at least 10 and will lose 2 population points to train 1 immigrant. The Immigrant will add 1 population point when joined to a city having a population of less than 7. The maintenance cost of 2 gold per turn ends when the immigrant joins a city.

name: Hashishin
pedia: The Hashishin were the legendary assassins of the 12th-century Middle East. Supposedly conditioned through religion and drugs into fanatics, these assassins would follow any order, no matter how extreme.

name: Shaolin Monk
pedia: The Shaolin Monks were the product of the Shaolin Temple. Their martial arts feats have become the stuff of legends across the globe.

Berserks (or berserkers) were Viking warriors who worked themselves up into terribles rage before going into battle. The word berserker comes from the Norse words "bjorn" (meaning bear) or "bare" (meaning naked) and "serkr" (meaning shirt); a berserk was either someone who went into battle wearing the "shirt" of a bear (i.e. someone who was bear-like in rage and strength) or someone who went into battle naked. Berserks thought that by wearing the fur of a bear they would be possessed by the spirit of the animal and possibly even shift into its form. Berserks were also known to have sworn allegiance to the Norse sky god Odin, and they believed that he would protect them and give them superhuman strength. The Vikings also believed that if death came while fighting as a berserk, the deceased would travel straight to the warrior paradise of Valhalla.[PARAGRAPH:2]No one is exactly sure how berserks induced the crazed state of violence described by contemporaries. Some theories speculate that the wild behavior was prompted by the taking of various hallucinogenic drugs, or was caused by mild epilepsy, or even a genetic disorder that caused overly large skull growth. Perhaps the most likely explanation is that before going into battle the berserks simply worked themselves into frenzies by painting their faces, howling like animals, beating helmets and drums - and drinking large quantities of alcohol.[PARAGRAPH:2]All stories about berserks agree that they fought with crazed strength and were immune to pain until battle was ended. Berserks were often portrayed as villains, slaying anything that moved - including their own family members. In battle even the Berserks' allies usually chose to keep them at arm's length and were never comfortable in their presence. Scandinavian kings refused to employ them as bodyguards for fear that they would forget who to protect in the fighting.[PARAGRAPH:2]In 1015, Berserks were outlawed in Norway, and Grágás, the medieval Icelandic law code, sentenced berserk warriors to exile. After the widespread adoption of Christianity across Scandinavia, in 1100 AD the organization of berserk warbands disappeared.

name: Spartacus
pedia: Spartacus (c. 109 BC-71 BC), according to Roman historians, was a slave and gladiator who became the leader (or possibly one of several leaders) in the somewhat successful slave uprising against the Roman Republic known as the Third Servile War. Little is known about Spartacus beyond the events of the war, and surviving historical accounts are inaccurate and often contradictory.[NEWLINE]Spartacus's struggle, often seen as the fight of an oppressed people fighting for their freedom against a slave-owning aristocracy, has found new meaning for modern writers since the 19th century. The rebellion of Spartacus has proven inspirational to many modern literary and political writers, making Spartacus a folk hero among cultures both ancient and modern.

name: Gladiator
pedia: In the time of Ancient Rome, gladiators were slaves put into service to fight.

name: Gatherer
pedia: A hunter-gatherer society is one whose primary subsistence method involves the direct procurement of edible plants and animals from the wild, foraging and hunting without significant recourse to the domestication of either. Hunter-gatherers obtain most from gathering rather than hunting; up to 80% of the food is obtained by gathering. The demarcation between hunter-gatherers and other societies which rely more upon domestication (see agriculture and pastoralism and neolithic revolution) is not clear-cut, as many contemporary societies combine both strategies to sustain their population.

name: Worker
pedia: The majority of people in all civilizations throughout time have been workers. Whether engaged in farming, commerce, or industry, their actions form the economic infrastructure without which no nation can succeed. Over time, the endless activity of workers has reshaped the very surface of the land by mining hills, cutting down huge stretches of forest, and irrigating hostile terrain into fertile farmland. By the late 20th century it had become apparent that excessive deforestation and pollution from heavy industry posed a threat to the world's environment.
